concepts.title: Trigonometric Equations
concepts.conceptBody:
Trigonometric Equations

A trigonometric equation is one that involves one or more trigonometric functions. For example,

$\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\text{t}+1=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\text{t}$

Solving trigonometric equations such as $2\text{sin}x=1$ refers to the process of finding the values for the variable $\text{x}$ that will make a true numerical statement. Since trigonometric functions are periodic most trigonometric equations have infinitely many solutions.

concepts.title: Root of Trigonometric Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Solution of a trigonometric equation

A value of the unknown angle which satisfies the given trigonometrical equation is called a solution or root of the equation.

Ex. $2\text{sin}\theta =1$, clearly $\theta =30^{\circ}$ and $\theta =150^{\circ}$ satisfies the equation, therefore, $30^{\circ}$ and $150^{\circ}$ are solutions of the equation $2\text{sin}\theta =1^{\circ}$ between $0^{\circ}$ and $180^{\circ}$.

concepts.title: Solution of a Trigonometric Equations
concepts.conceptBody:
Solution of a trigonometric equation

A trigonometric equation has three kinds of solutions : 

  1. Principal solution   : Numerically smallest value of the unknown angle satisfying the given equation.
  2. Particular solution : Any value of angle satisfying the given equation.
  3. General solution    : Collection of all particular solutions.

For example, the equation $\text{cot}\left( \theta -\pi /6 \right)=\sqrt{3}$ is satisfied

If $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta -\frac{\pi }{6}=\frac{\pi }{6}$ or $\frac{7\pi }{6}$ or $-\frac{5\pi }{6}$, etc.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\pi }{3},\frac{4\pi }{3},-\frac{2\pi }{3}$, etc.

Out of these, the numerically smallest is $\theta =\pi /3$.

This is the principal solution. It is a particular solution also. Also the above values of $\theta $ taken individually are particular solutions.

For the general solution, we observe that since cotangent function is periodic with period $\pi $ and it takes the value $\sqrt{3}$ only once in the internal $\left[ 0,\pi \right],\left( \theta -\pi /6 \right)$ can be equal to any particular solution plus a multiple of $\pi $. If we take the particular solution as the principal solution,

Then $\theta -\frac{\pi }{6}=\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{6},\text{n}\in 1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{3},\text{n}\in 1$ is the general solution.

If in any equation, we get two values of the unknown angle which are numerically equal but opposite in sign, the principal solution is assumed as the positive angle, e.g., Principal solution of $\text{sec}\theta =2$ is $\theta =\pi /3$, although $\theta =-\pi /3$ also satisfies it.

concepts.title: Solution of Some Simple Trigonometric Equations
General Solution of Trigonometric Equations
concepts.conceptBody:
General Solution

Since trigonometrical functions are periodic functions, therefore, solutions of trigonometrical equations can be generalized with the help of periodicity of trigonometrical functions. The solution consisting of all possible solutions of a trigonometrical equation is called its general solution.

General solution of equation 

(a) \(\sin \theta=\sin \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \alpha ; \quad \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}\)
\(\begin{aligned} \sin \theta=\mathrm{k},-1 \leq \mathrm{k} \leq 1 \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \alpha & ; \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I} . \text { and } \alpha=\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{k} \\ & \text { (finding principal value of } \alpha \text {) }\end{aligned}\)
(b) \(\cos \theta=\cos \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad  \theta=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha \quad ; \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}\)
\(\cos \theta=\mathrm{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha \quad ; \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I} \text { and } \alpha=\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{k}\)
                                               (where \(\alpha\) is the principal angle)
(c) \(\tan \theta=\tan \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+\alpha \quad ; \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}\)
\(\tan \theta=\mathrm{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+\alpha \quad ; \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}, \alpha=\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{k}\)
                                             (where \(\alpha\) is the principal angle)
(d) \(\sin ^2 \theta=\sin ^2 \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=n \pi \pm \alpha\)
(e) \(\cos ^2 \theta=\cos ^2 \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha\)
(f) \(\tan ^2 \theta=\tan ^2 \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha \mathrm{d}\)

Some deductions: 

(I) 1. $\text{sin}\theta =0\Rightarrow \theta =\text{n}\pi $

2. $\text{cos}\theta =0\Rightarrow \theta =\left( 4n\pm 1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$

3. $\text{tan}\theta =0\Rightarrow \theta =\text{n}\pi $

(II) 1. $\text{sin}\theta =1\Rightarrow \theta =\left( 4\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$

2. $\text{cos}\theta =1\Rightarrow \theta =2\text{n}\pi $

(III) 1. $\text{sin}\theta =-1\Rightarrow \theta =2\text{n}\pi +\frac{3\pi }{2}$

2. $\text{cos}\theta =-1\Rightarrow \theta =\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\pi $

concepts.title: Particular Solution of Trigonometric Equations
concepts.conceptBody:
Particular Solution

All possible values of unknown which satisfy the given equation are called solutions of the given equation.

For complete Solution: 

  1. There should be no extraneous root.
  2. There should be no root loss.

While solving equations following points must be kept in mind. 

  1. Squaring should be avoided as far as possible. If squaring is done check for the extraneous roots.
  2. Never cancel equal terms containing 'unknown' on two sides which are in product. It may cause root loss.
  3. The answer should not contain such values of $\theta $ which make any of the terms undefined.
  4. Domain should not change. If it changes, necessary corrections must be made.
  5. Check that denominator is not zero at any stage while solving equations.

concepts.title: Problem Based on Simplification
concepts.conceptBody:
Problems based on preliminaries 

In this type we will discuss problems which can be converted in standard form after a little simplification.

Q) Find the most general values of $\theta $ satisfying the equations : 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}\theta =-1 & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (ii) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}\theta =-\frac{1}{2} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (iii) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tan}\theta =-\sqrt{3} \\ \end{array}$ 

Solution : 

(i) Given equation is $\text{sin}\theta =-1$.

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}\theta =\text{sin}\left( -\frac{\pi }{2} \right)\Rightarrow \theta =\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\left( -\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

i.e. $\theta =\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}+1}}\frac{\pi }{2}$, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

(ii) Given equation is $\text{cos}\theta =-\frac{1}{2}$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}\theta =\text{cos}\frac{2\pi }{3}\Rightarrow \theta =2\text{n}\pi \pm \frac{2\pi }{3}$ where $\text{n}=0.\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

(iii) Given equation is $\text{tan}\theta =-\sqrt{3}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tan}\theta =\text{tan}\left( -\frac{\pi }{3} \right)\Rightarrow \theta =\text{n}\pi +\left( -\frac{\pi }{3} \right) \\ {} & ~=\text{n}\pi -\frac{\pi }{3},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ where }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \\ \end{array}$

Q) Solve the equation : 

(i) $\text{sin}9\theta =\text{sin}\theta $ (ii) $\text{sin}5x=\text{cos}2x$ 

Solution : 

(i) Given Equation is $\text{sin}9\theta =\text{sin}\theta $

or, $\text{sin}9\theta -\text{sin}\theta =0$

or, $2\text{cos}\frac{9\theta +\theta }{2}\cdot \text{sin}\frac{9\theta -\theta }{2}=0$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}5\theta \cdot \text{sin}4\theta =0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ either $\text{cos}5\theta =0$ or, sin 4θ = 0

$\Rightarrow 5\theta =\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$ ⟹ 4θ = nπ

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\left( 2n+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{10}$ ∴ θ = $\frac{n\pi }{4}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\text{n}\pi }{4},\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{10}$ where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

(ii) Given equation is

$\text{sin}5\text{x}=\text{cos}2\text{x}$.

or, $\text{cos}2x=\text{cos}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-5x \right)$

$\Rightarrow 2\text{x}=2\text{n}\pi \pm \left( \frac{\pi }{2}-5\text{x} \right)$

Taking positive sign, $2\text{x}=2\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{2}-5\text{x}$.

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }7\text{x}=2\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{x}=\left( 4\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{14}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$

Taking negative sign, $2\text{x}=2\text{n}\pi -\frac{\pi }{2}+5\text{x}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-3x=\left( 4n-1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore x=-\left( 4n-1 \right)\frac{\pi }{6}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$

Hence $\text{x}=\left( 4\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{14},-\left( 4\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\pi }{6}$ where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

Q) Solve $3\text{tan}\left( \theta -15 \right)=\text{tan}\left( \theta +15 \right)$ 

Solution : 

Given, $3\text{tan}\left( \theta -15 \right)=\text{tan}\left( \theta +15 \right)$

or, $\frac{\text{tan}\left( \theta +{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}{\text{tan}\left( \theta -{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}=\frac{3}{1}$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{tan}\left( \theta +{{15}^{\circ }} \right)+\text{tan}\left( \theta -{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}{\text{tran}\left( \theta +{{15}^{\circ }} \right)-\text{tan}\left( \theta -{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}=\frac{4}{2}$ [By componendo and dividendo]

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{sin}\left( \theta +{{15}^{\circ }}+\theta -{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}{\text{sin}\left( \theta +{{15}^{\circ }}-\theta +{{15}^{\circ }} \right)}=2$

or, $2\text{sin}2\theta =2$ or, $\text{sin}2\theta =1=\text{sin}\frac{\pi }{2}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\theta =\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\frac{\pi }{2}$ $\therefore \theta =\frac{\text{n}\pi }{2}+{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\frac{\pi }{4}$

where $n=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

concepts.title: Problem Based on Factorisation
concepts.conceptBody:
Problems based on method of factorization

Working Rule : 

Step I. Make R.H.S. zero, factorize L.H.S.

Step II. Equate each factor to zero and solve.

Step III. Check for root loss and extraneous roots.

Finally, collect the results after correction.

Q) Solve, $7\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +3\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta =4$ 

Solution : 

Given $7\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +3\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta =4$

or, $7\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +3\left( 1-\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta \right)=4$

or, $4\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta =1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{cos}\theta =\pm \frac{1}{3}$

Taking positive sign, $\text{cos}\theta =\frac{1}{2}=\text{cos}\frac{\pi }{3}\Rightarrow \theta =2\text{n}\pi \pm \frac{\pi }{3}$

Taking negative sign, $\text{cos}\theta =-\frac{1}{2}=\text{cos}\frac{2\pi }{3}\Rightarrow \theta =2\text{n}\pi \pm \frac{2\pi }{3}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =2\text{n}\pi \pm \frac{\pi }{3},2\text{n}\pi \pm \frac{2\pi }{3}$, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

Q) Solve $2\text{cos}2\theta +\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }=2$ 

Solution : 

$\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }=2\left( 1-\text{cos}2\theta \right)$

or, $\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }=4\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or, $\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }-4\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta =0$

or, $\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }\left[ 1-2\sqrt{2}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{\frac{3}{2}}}\theta \right]=0$

If $\sqrt{2\text{sin}\theta }=0,\text{sin}\theta =0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ .\sqrt{\text{z}}=0\Rightarrow \text{z}=0 \right]$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\text{n}\pi $, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

If $1-2\sqrt{2}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3/2}}\theta =0,\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3/2}}\theta =\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\text{sin}\theta )}^{3/2}}={{\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}\Rightarrow \text{sin}\theta =\frac{1}{2}=\text{sin}\frac{\pi }{6}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\frac{\pi }{6}$, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\text{n}\pi ,\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\frac{\pi }{6}$, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

or, $\frac{\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta -\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta }{\text{cos}\theta \text{sin}\theta }-\left( \text{cos}\theta -\text{sin}\theta \right)=0$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{cos}\theta -\text{sin}\theta \right)\left[ \frac{\text{cos}\theta +\text{sin}\theta }{\text{cos}\theta \text{sin}\theta } \right]=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ either $\text{cos}\theta -\text{sin}\theta =0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or, $\frac{\text{cos}\theta +\text{sin}\theta }{\text{sin}\theta \text{cos}\theta }-1=0$

If $\text{cos}\theta -\text{sin}\theta =0,\text{cos}\theta =\text{sin}\theta $

or, $\text{tan}\theta =1$ or, $\text{tan}\theta =\text{tan}\frac{\pi }{4}\Rightarrow \theta =\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{4}$

If $\frac{\text{cos}\theta +\text{sin}\theta }{\text{sin}\theta \text{cos}\theta }-1=0$

$\text{cos}\theta +\text{sin}\theta =\text{sin}\theta \text{cos}\theta $

or, $\text{cos}\theta +\text{sin}\theta =\frac{1}{2}\text{sin}2\theta $

Squaring, we get $1+\text{sin}2\theta =\frac{1}{4}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}2\theta $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}2\theta =\frac{4\pm \sqrt{16-4.1\left( -4 \right)}}{2}$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}2\theta =2\pm 2\sqrt{2}$

Taking positive sign, $\text{sin}2\theta =2\pm 2\sqrt{2}>1$ and hence impossible

Taking negative sign, $\text{sin}2\theta =2-2\sqrt{2}=\text{sin}\alpha $ (say)

[ . $\left| 2-2\sqrt{2} \right|<1$, hence we have supposed it as $\text{sin}\alpha $ ]

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\theta =\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\alpha $

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\text{n}\pi }{2}+{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\frac{\alpha }{2}$, where $\alpha =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( 2-2\sqrt{2} \right)$

where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $

concepts.title: Position Vector
concepts.conceptBody:
Position vector of a point

If $\text{O}$ be fixed origin & $\text{P}$ be any point then vector $\overline{\text{OP}}$ is called the position vector of point $P$.

i.e. $\overline{\text{OP}}=x\tilde{i}+y\tilde{j}+z\tilde{k}$

where $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ are real (scalar) numbers & are $\text{X},\text{Y},\text{Z}$ components of vector $\bar{p}$.

So other representation is

$\overline{\text{p}}={{\text{p}}_{\text{x}}}\widetilde{\text{i}}+{{\text{p}}_{y}}\widetilde{\text{j}}+{{\text{p}}_{z}}\widetilde{\text{k}}$

To find $\overline{\text{AB}}$:

We have to find $\overline{\text{AB}}$, given that position vectors of $\text{A}\,\,\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\,\,\text{B}$ are $\overline{\text{a}}\,\,\And \,\,\overline{\text{b}}$ respectively.

i.e. $\overline{\text{OA}}=\overline{\text{a}}$

$\overline{\text{OB}}=\overline{\text{b}}$

in $\Delta \text{OAB}\,\,\,\,\overline{\text{OA}}+\overline{\text{AB}}=\text{OB}$

putting values

$\Rightarrow \quad \overline{\text{AB}}=\overline{\text{OB}}-\overline{\text{OA}}$

$=\overline{\text{b}}-\overline{\text{a}}$

TIP to remember 

$\text{ also if }\quad \bar{a}={{a}_{x}}\tilde{i}+{{a}_{y}}\tilde{j}+{{a}_{2}}\tilde{k}\quad \text{ and }\quad \bar{b}={{b}_{x}}\tilde{i}+{{b}_{y}}\tilde{j}+{{b}_{z}}\tilde{k}$

$\text{ then }\quad \overline{\text{AB}}=\left( {{b}_{\text{x}}}-{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}} \right)\widetilde{\text{i}}+\left( {{\text{b}}_{\text{y}}}-{{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}} \right)\widetilde{\text{j}}+\left( {{\text{b}}_{\text{z}}}-{{\text{a}}_{\text{z}}} \right)\widetilde{\text{k}}$
